questions.text: According to the ACFE, what is the primary purpose of horizontal analysis when examining financial statements during a fraud investigation?
options: A. To compare each line item on the financial statement as a percentage of a base year amount to identify unusual trends or anomalies over time
B. To evaluate the proportional relationship between assets and liabilities on a single balance sheet
C. To calculate the company's profitability margins relative to industry benchmarks
D. To assess the liquidity position by comparing current assets to current liabilities
questions.tag: Financial Analysis Methods — Horizontal Analysis

questions.text: Which of the following best defines 'accounts receivable aging' in the context of fraud examination?
options: A. The process of categorizing outstanding customer invoices by the length of time they have remained unpaid
B. The depreciation method used to reduce the book value of receivables over their estimated useful life
C. The adjustment of receivables to fair market value at the end of each fiscal period
D. The classification of customers based on their creditworthiness and payment history
questions.tag: Asset Analysis — Accounts Receivable

questions.text: In accounting standards, what does the term 'materiality' primarily refer to?
options: A. The threshold at which an error or omission in financial statements could influence the economic decisions of a reasonable user
B. The physical composition and durability of fixed assets recorded on the balance sheet
C. The requirement that all transactions be supported by tangible documentation
D. The classification of assets based on their composition (tangible vs. intangible)
questions.tag: Accounting Principles — Materiality Concept

questions.text: Which ratio is specifically designed to measure how efficiently a company collects cash from its credit customers?
options: A. Days Sales Outstanding (DSO)
B. Inventory Turnover Ratio
C. Return on Assets (ROA)
D. Debt-to-Equity Ratio
questions.tag: Financial Ratios — Receivables Efficiency

questions.text: According to accounting standards, what is the definition of 'subsequent events' in financial reporting?
options: A. Events occurring between the balance sheet date and the date the financial statements are issued that may require adjustment or disclosure
B. All transactions that occur after the fiscal year-end but before the next fiscal year begins
C. Future transactions that are reasonably expected to occur based on current economic conditions
D. Events related to the company's operations that occurred during the audit period
questions.tag: Financial Reporting Standards — Subsequent Events

questions.text: In the context of fraud detection, what does the term 'analytical anomaly' most accurately describe?
options: A. An unusual relationship or pattern in financial data that deviates significantly from expected results or historical trends
B. Any variance between budgeted and actual financial results exceeding 10 percent
C. A deliberate misstatement of financial information by management
D. A difference in accounting methods between consecutive reporting periods
questions.tag: Fraud Detection — Analytical Anomalies

questions.text: Which of the following best defines 'goodwill' as it appears on a company's balance sheet?
options: A. An intangible asset representing the excess amount paid for an acquired company over the fair value of its identifiable net assets
B. The accumulated profits retained by a company at the end of the fiscal period
C. A reserve account established to cover potential bad debts from credit sales
D. The premium paid for long-term debt instruments issued by the company
questions.tag: Balance Sheet Components — Intangible Assets

questions.text: According to accounting standards, what is 'depreciation' most accurately defined as?
options: A. The systematic allocation of the cost of a tangible fixed asset over its estimated useful life
B. The reduction in market value of an asset due to economic conditions
C. The physical deterioration of equipment or property that requires immediate replacement
D. The annual adjustment to asset values based on current fair market prices
questions.tag: Asset Valuation — Depreciation Concept

questions.text: What does the term 'revenue recognition principle' require according to generally accepted accounting principles?
options: A. Revenue should be recognized when the performance obligation is satisfied and the company has the right to receive consideration
B. Revenue must be recognized only when cash is physically received from the customer
C. Revenue is recognized at the end of each fiscal year regardless of when services are performed
D. Revenue should be recognized proportionally throughout the contract period regardless of delivery
questions.tag: Accounting Principles — Revenue Recognition

questions.text: In the context of financial analysis, what does 'leverage' primarily measure?
options: A. The degree to which a company uses debt financing relative to equity financing
B. The efficiency with which a company converts inventory into sales
C. The percentage of operating expenses relative to total revenue
D. The rate at which a company's assets are depreciating over time
questions.tag: Financial Analysis — Leverage & Solvency

questions.text: Which accounting concept states that a company should record transactions and prepare financial statements assuming the entity will continue to operate indefinitely?
options: A. Going Concern Assumption
B. Matching Principle
C. Conservatism Convention
D. Consistency Principle
questions.tag: Accounting Assumptions — Going Concern

questions.text: According to the ACFE, what is meant by 'related party transactions' in financial reporting?
options: A. Transactions between the company and individuals or entities that have a relationship with the company that may affect independence or objectivity
B. All transactions involving multiple departments within the same company
C. Purchases and sales made only with direct competitors in the industry
D. Transactions that occur between the company and its external auditors
questions.tag: Financial Reporting Disclosures — Related Parties

questions.text: Which of the following best defines 'allowance for doubtful accounts' in the context of receivables analysis?
options: A. A contra-asset account that estimates the portion of accounts receivable that may not be collected based on historical experience and current conditions
B. A temporary holding account for customer payments that are pending verification
C. The total amount of credit extended to customers during the fiscal year
D. A reserve fund established by management to cover unexpected operational expenses
questions.tag: Asset Valuation — Receivables Estimation

questions.text: What is the key distinction between the allowance method and the direct write-off method for accounting for uncollectible accounts receivable?
options: A. The allowance method estimates bad debts in advance using a contra-asset account, while the direct write-off method recognizes losses only when accounts are confirmed uncollectible
B. The allowance method is used by small businesses, while the direct write-off method is required for large corporations under GAAP
C. The direct write-off method provides better matching of revenues and expenses compared to the allowance method
D. Both methods produce identical results on the income statement but differ only in balance sheet presentation
questions.tag: Receivables Valuation — Uncollectible Accounts Recognition

questions.text: Which of the following best distinguishes between operating activities and investing activities in a statement of cash flows?
options: A. Operating activities include all purchases and sales of assets, while investing activities relate only to financing decisions
B. Operating activities involve day-to-day business transactions that affect net income, while investing activities involve purchases and sales of long-term assets and investments
C. Investing activities always generate positive cash flows, while operating activities may generate negative cash flows during growth periods
D. Operating activities are non-recurring events, while investing activities occur regularly throughout the fiscal year
questions.tag: Cash Flow Classification — Activity Categories

questions.text: When comparing straight-line depreciation to the double-declining-balance depreciation method, which statement is most accurate?
options: A. Straight-line depreciation allocates equal amounts of expense each period, while double-declining-balance accelerates depreciation expense in earlier periods
B. Double-declining-balance results in lower total depreciation expense over the asset's life than straight-line depreciation
C. Straight-line depreciation is only permitted under IFRS, while double-declining-balance is required under GAAP
D. Both methods result in identical book values at the end of an asset's useful life regardless of which method is chosen
questions.tag: Fixed Asset Depreciation — Method Comparison

questions.text: What is the fundamental difference between how FIFO (First-In, First-Out) and LIFO (Last-In, First-Out) inventory methods affect financial statements during periods of rising prices?
options: A. FIFO results in higher cost of goods sold and lower net income, while LIFO results in lower cost of goods sold and higher net income
B. LIFO results in higher cost of goods sold and lower net income, while FIFO results in lower cost of goods sold and higher net income
C. Both methods produce identical net income but differ in inventory valuation on the balance sheet
D. FIFO is prohibited under IFRS, while LIFO is the only acceptable method under GAAP during inflationary periods
questions.tag: Inventory Valuation — Cost Flow Methods

questions.text: Which distinction between a liability and an expense is most critical for a fraud examiner to understand when detecting financial statement manipulation?
options: A. Liabilities represent future obligations while expenses represent costs incurred in generating revenue during the current period
B. Liabilities always require cash payments, while expenses may be recorded without any cash outflow
C. Expenses are permanent reductions in equity, while liabilities are temporary obligations that always reverse
D. Liabilities appear only on the income statement, while expenses appear only on the balance sheet
questions.tag: Balance Sheet Elements — Liability vs. Expense Distinction

questions.text: A company reports a gross profit margin of 45% while its industry average is 28%. Which statement best describes what this comparison suggests?
options: A. The company is likely more efficient at controlling cost of goods sold, though further investigation is warranted to identify the underlying causes
B. The company has definitely engaged in revenue inflation or understatement of inventory costs, indicating fraud
C. The company's pricing strategy is less competitive than its peers and will result in market share loss
D. This discrepancy is immaterial and requires no further investigation as it falls within normal business variation
questions.tag: Profitability Analysis — Gross Margin Anomalies

questions.text: What is the key difference between how accounts receivable aging analysis and the accounts receivable turnover ratio serve different purposes in fraud detection?
options: A. Aging analysis identifies which specific accounts may be uncollectible or fictitious, while turnover ratio reveals the overall efficiency of receivables collection relative to industry norms
B. Aging analysis is used only for valuation purposes on the balance sheet, while turnover ratio is used exclusively for income statement analysis
C. Turnover ratio detects fictitious sales, while aging analysis only measures collection efficiency
D. Both tools measure identical concepts but use different mathematical formulas to arrive at the same conclusion
questions.tag: Receivables Analysis — Detection Methodologies

questions.text: How does capitalizing an expenditure versus expensing it immediately create different financial statement impacts, and why is this distinction crucial in forensic accounting?
options: A. Capitalizing inflates assets and net income in the current period, while expensing reduces both; manipulating this distinction can fraudulently alter reported profitability and financial position
B. Expensing an item always results in higher assets and net income, while capitalizing always reduces both figures
C. The choice between capitalization and expensing has no impact on the balance sheet, only on the income statement
D. Capitalizing and expensing produce identical results; the difference is purely a presentational matter with no financial impact
questions.tag: Asset Capitalization — Fraud Risk Manipulation

questions.text: Which best describes the difference between a classified balance sheet and a comparative balance sheet in the context of detecting unusual account movements?
options: A. A classified balance sheet organizes accounts by liquidity and shows current versus non-current categories, while a comparative balance sheet displays multiple periods side-by-side to reveal changes and trends
B. A classified balance sheet is used by manufacturing companies, while a comparative balance sheet is used only by service companies
C. Both are identical formats; the terms are used interchangeably in financial reporting standards
D. A comparative balance sheet is required under GAAP but prohibited under IFRS for fraud detection purposes
questions.tag: Balance Sheet Format — Analytical Application

questions.text: What is the primary difference between using horizontal analysis and vertical analysis when examining a company's financial statements for potential fraud indicators?
options: A. Horizontal analysis examines changes across multiple periods to identify unusual trends, while vertical analysis evaluates the relative size of items within a single period as a percentage of a base amount
B. Horizontal analysis is performed on the balance sheet only, while vertical analysis is performed only on the income statement
C. Vertical analysis identifies fraud more effectively because it uses percentages, while horizontal analysis is purely descriptive
D. Both analyses measure the same relationships; they are simply different names for the same analytical procedure
questions.tag: Financial Statement Analysis — Comparative Methodologies

questions.text: How does the treatment of contingent liabilities differ between GAAP and IFRS, and why should a fraud examiner understand this distinction?
options: A. GAAP requires accrual of probable contingencies, while IFRS requires disclosure only; understanding these differences prevents misinterpreting non-compliance as intentional understatement of liabilities
B. IFRS requires accrual of all contingencies regardless of probability, while GAAP permits complete non-disclosure
C. Both standards require identical treatment; any discrepancy indicates fraud was committed
D. GAAP permits accrual of contingencies at management's discretion, while IFRS prohibits all contingency accounting
questions.tag: Contingent Liabilities — GAAP vs. IFRS Standards

questions.text: When analyzing a company's debt-to-equity ratio, which comparison is most useful for identifying potential financial distress or fraudulent misstatements of financial condition?
options: A. Comparing the company's current ratio to its debt-to-equity ratio reveals whether liquidity problems exist
B. Comparing the company's ratio to industry benchmarks and prior-year trends reveals whether leverage has increased abnormally, potentially indicating hidden debt or inflated equity values
C. Comparing total debt to total assets provides the same information as the debt-to-equity ratio
D. The debt-to-equity ratio alone is sufficient; no comparison to other metrics or periods is necessary
questions.tag: Leverage Analysis — Comparative Benchmarking

questions.text: What is the essential distinction between how revenue recognition timing differs under the percentage-of-completion method versus the completed-contract method for long-term contracts?
options: A. Percentage-of-completion recognizes revenue proportionally as work progresses, while completed-contract recognizes all revenue only when the contract is finished; fraudsters prefer the latter to delay income recognition
B. Completed-contract method produces higher revenue recognition each period than percentage-of-completion
C. Both methods recognize identical amounts of revenue; they differ only in the timing of cash collection
D. Percentage-of-completion is prohibited under both GAAP and IFRS for construction contracts
questions.tag: Revenue Recognition — Long-Term Contract Methods

questions.text: During a fraud examination of a manufacturing company, a CFE notices that accounts receivable increased by 45% while revenue only increased by 8% over the same period. Which of the following is the most likely red flag this pattern suggests?
options: A. The company is experiencing normal seasonal fluctuations in collections
B. Revenue recognition fraud or channel stuffing may be occurring, requiring further investigation into sales cutoff and customer creditworthiness
C. The company has legitimately extended credit terms to attract new customers
D. Accounts receivable aging has improved, indicating better collection practices
questions.tag: Revenue Recognition Fraud Detection — Accounts Receivable Anomalies

questions.text: A CFE is examining a company's cost of goods sold (COGS) and observes that COGS as a percentage of revenue decreased from 62% to 48% without any documented operational changes or efficiency improvements. What should the examiner's primary concern be?
options: A. The company has successfully reduced waste in its manufacturing process
B. Inventory may be overstated, obsolete inventory may be unwritten, or COGS may be underreported through false inventory cutoffs
C. The company has renegotiated supplier contracts at more favorable rates
D. Revenue growth indicates strong market demand and improved operational leverage
questions.tag: Inventory Fraud Detection — COGS Manipulation

questions.text: During a cash flow analysis, a CFE discovers that a company reported operating cash flow of $2.8 million while net income was only $1.2 million, primarily due to a $1.6 million decrease in accounts payable. Which control would best have prevented or detected potential fraud in this scenario?
options: A. Implementing a policy requiring vendor confirmations and reconciliation of accounts payable aging reports on a quarterly basis
B. Increasing the frequency of physical inventory counts
C. Strengthening the revenue recognition policy and customer credit reviews
D. Enhancing the depreciation method documentation and fixed asset register
questions.tag: Cash Flow Fraud Prevention — Accounts Payable Manipulation Controls

questions.text: A CFE examining journal entries discovers numerous adjusting entries posted by the accounting manager in the final days of the fiscal year that reverse earlier deferred revenue transactions. The amounts are immaterial individually but significant in aggregate. What is the examiner's most appropriate next step?
options: A. Accept the entries as standard period-end adjustments and close the books
B. Investigate the business purpose, authorization, and supporting documentation for each reversal to assess for revenue manipulation and potential fraud
C. Request that the entries be reposted at the beginning of the next fiscal period
D. Compare the entries to similar adjustments made in prior years to establish a pattern
questions.tag: Journal Entry Fraud Detection — Period-End Adjustments Analysis

questions.text: While analyzing a company's fixed assets, a CFE notes that the accumulated depreciation percentage (accumulated depreciation ÷ gross fixed assets) is significantly lower than industry benchmarks, while the company's asset age appears comparable to competitors. What is the most likely explanation requiring investigation?
options: A. The company is using accelerated depreciation methods appropriate for its asset mix
B. The company may have capitalized routine maintenance expenses or extended asset useful lives inappropriately to inflate net asset values
C. The company purchased newer assets more recently than competitors
D. The industry benchmarks are not applicable due to differences in accounting standards
questions.tag: Fixed Asset Fraud Detection — Depreciation Policy Anomalies

questions.text: A CFE is reviewing a company's allowance for doubtful accounts and observes that despite a 35% increase in accounts receivable aging over 90 days, the allowance for doubtful accounts decreased by 12% year-over-year. Which red flag does this pattern most strongly indicate?
options: A. The credit department has successfully improved collection efforts for aged receivables
B. The company may be understating the allowance for doubtful accounts to inflate net receivables and earnings, potentially constituting financial statement fraud
C. The company has adopted a more conservative accounting policy aligned with IFRS standards
D. The aging of receivables is due to seasonal factors unrelated to collectibility
questions.tag: Allowance for Doubtful Accounts Fraud — Earnings Manipulation

questions.text: During a detailed examination of intercompany transactions, a CFE discovers that Company A (a subsidiary) sold inventory to Company B (the parent) at a 40% markup above fair market value near year-end, with Company B recording the inventory but not making payment until 45 days after the period ended. What is the primary fraud concern?
options: A. This represents a legitimate management decision to optimize working capital between related entities
B. Inflated intercompany sales revenue, overstated inventory, and potentially disguised related-party financing arrangements that may violate consolidation principles and misstate earnings
C. The parent company is appropriately deferring payment to preserve cash flow
D. The transaction demonstrates appropriate transfer pricing policies
questions.tag: Related-Party Transaction Fraud — Intercompany Revenue Manipulation

questions.text: A CFE examining employee expense reimbursements notices that one manager's monthly submissions average $8,400, while comparable managers average $3,200. The documentation appears complete and properly authorized. What should be the examiner's approach?
options: A. Accept the expenses as properly documented and authorized
B. Request itemized receipts and investigate the specific nature of expenses, compare to company policy, and interview the manager regarding unusual business justifications for the discrepancy
C. Automatically deny all expenses exceeding the departmental average
D. Report the manager to law enforcement without further investigation
questions.tag: Expense Reimbursement Fraud Detection — Outlier Analysis

questions.text: An examiner identifies that a company's sales return rate is 3.2% compared to the industry average of 0.8%, but no allowance for sales returns has been established in the current period. Revenue has been recorded at gross invoice amounts. Which accounting concept is violated, and what is the fraud concern?
options: A. The matching principle is violated; the company is understating current period expenses, overstating revenue, and may be masking quality issues
B. The conservatism principle suggests gross revenue recording is appropriate without estimation
C. The materiality principle allows for non-recognition of amounts below 5% of net income
D. The substance-over-form principle requires recording at the net amount only for cash sales
questions.tag: Sales Returns and Allowances Fraud — Revenue Overstatement

questions.text: During a bank reconciliation review, a CFE discovers ten checks totaling $287,000 that were recorded in the accounting records 6–8 weeks before they were actually presented to the bank. These checks remain outstanding. What is the most significant concern?
options: A. The accounting department is using standard accounting procedures with normal clearing delays
B. Check kiting, cash manipulation, or potential misappropriation may be occurring; the timing suggests deliberate misstatement of cash balances
C. The checks represent legitimate prepaid expenses that will clear in the subsequent period
D. The bank is processing checks slower than normal due to operational issues
questions.tag: Cash Management Fraud Detection — Outstanding Check Manipulation

questions.text: A CFE is analyzing the relationship between days sales outstanding (DSO) and the aging of accounts receivable. The DSO has remained constant at 42 days, but the percentage of receivables over 90 days has increased from 8% to 24%. What does this pattern most likely indicate?
options: A. The company has improved its overall collection performance
B. New customers with longer payment terms have increased sales, offsetting the older aged accounts
C. Revenue has been manipulated by recording sales from fictitious or uncreditworthy customers, while selective collection focus on recent sales maintains DSO stability
D. The company's industry is experiencing extended payment cycles across all market participants
questions.tag: Accounts Receivable Fraud Detection — Aging Pattern Anomalies

questions.text: A company's balance sheet shows intangible assets and goodwill increasing from $4.2 million to $12.8 million within one fiscal year, with a corresponding note disclosing multiple acquisition transactions. However, no impairment testing documentation is available. Which control would best detect potential fraudulent asset valuation?
options: A. Requiring independent valuation expert reviews for all intangible asset and goodwill acquisitions, with documented annual impairment assessments and management sign-off
B. Implementing a policy requiring only internal management estimation of goodwill values
C. Accepting management representations regarding acquisition valuations without third-party verification
D. Deferring impairment testing until the assets decline significantly in market value
questions.tag: Goodwill and Intangible Asset Fraud Prevention — Valuation Controls

questions.text: A CFE examining a company's lease accounting discovers that the company has classified a 7-year equipment lease with payments of $125,000 annually as an operating lease, recording only $125,000 annual rent expense. The equipment's fair value is $650,000. Under current accounting standards (ASC 842 / IFRS 16), what is the primary fraud indicator?
options: A. The classification is appropriate for a 7-year lease of equipment
B. Operating lease treatment is correct because payments are fixed and predictable
C. The lease should likely be capitalized as a right-of-use asset; the operating lease classification may be fraudulent misclassification to understate liabilities and overstate earnings
D. The equipment's fair value is irrelevant to lease classification decisions
questions.tag: Lease Accounting Fraud Detection — Misclassification and Liability Understatement

questions.text: During a fraud examination of a manufacturing company, a CFE discovers that cost of goods sold (COGS) increased by 45% while revenue increased by only 8% over the same period. Upon investigation, the examiner finds that the company capitalized certain routine maintenance expenses as part of inventory. Which conclusion is most supported by this evidence?
options: A. The company's gross profit margin legitimately declined due to increased raw material costs in the market.
B. The company intentionally inflated inventory values by capitalizing expenses that should have been expensed, thereby understating COGS and overstating net income.
C. The company's production efficiency has improved significantly, justifying the higher inventory balances on the balance sheet.
D. The disproportionate increase in COGS reflects normal seasonal variations in manufacturing operations.
questions.tag: Inventory Valuation & Capitalization — Detection of Asset Inflation

questions.text: A CFE is analyzing a company's accounts receivable aging schedule and notes that 60% of receivables are over 90 days old, yet the allowance for doubtful accounts remains at only 2% of total receivables. The company's historical bad debt write-off rate has averaged 8% annually. Which combination of factors best explains a potential fraud indicator?
options: A. The company is maintaining an inadequate allowance reserve while carrying significantly aged receivables, suggesting potential revenue overstatement and earnings manipulation.
B. The aging schedule is an outdated report that no longer reflects the current quality of the receivables portfolio.
C. The 2% allowance is appropriate because the company has implemented more rigorous collection procedures in recent periods.
D. The high percentage of aged receivables indicates a temporary cash flow issue unrelated to asset quality or valuation.
questions.tag: Accounts Receivable & Allowance Estimates — Analytical Anomalies

questions.text: A CFE examining a company's depreciation policy discovers that the company changed its depreciation method from straight-line to accelerated depreciation midway through the fiscal year for all assets acquired in the prior year, without adequate disclosure in the financial statements. Under both GAAP and IFRS, what is the primary concern regarding this accounting treatment?
options: A. Accelerated depreciation methods are not permitted under either GAAP or IFRS and represent a clear accounting violation.
B. While the method change itself may be acceptable, the lack of retroactive restatement and inadequate disclosure violates the consistency principle and full disclosure requirements.
C. The midyear change in depreciation method is standard practice in manufacturing industries and requires no special disclosure.
D. GAAP and IFRS specifically prohibit changes in depreciation methods once assets are placed in service.
questions.tag: Depreciation & Asset Accounting — Policy Changes & Disclosure

questions.text: During a forensic analysis, a CFE identifies that a company's operating cash flow has remained relatively flat at $2.2 million for three consecutive years, while reported net income has grown from $1.8 million to $3.1 million over the same period. The quality of earnings ratio shows a declining trend. Which conclusion is most supported regarding the reliability of reported earnings?
options: A. The company's earnings quality is strong, as net income growth significantly exceeds operating cash flow, indicating successful revenue generation.

B. The divergence between net income growth and stagnant operating cash flow suggests potential earnings manipulation through accruals, possibly indicating aggressive revenue recognition or inflated receivables.
C. The flat operating cash flow is attributable to efficient working capital management, which is a positive indicator of operational performance.
D. The company is likely distributing excess earnings to shareholders through dividends, explaining why cash flow has not increased proportionally.
questions.tag: Cash Flow Quality & Earnings Management — Operating vs. Net Income Analysis

questions.text: A CFE is evaluating a company's financial statements and observes that while the balance sheet shows total assets of $50 million and total liabilities of $30 million, the statement of cash flows indicates negative free cash flow of $(3.5) million for the period. Additionally, the company paid dividends of $2 million. Which analytical finding most warrants further investigation?
options: A. The company is financially distressed because negative free cash flow combined with dividend payments indicates unsustainable operations.
B. This scenario is consistent with a mature, profitable company investing in growth while returning capital to shareholders.

C. The negative free cash flow in conjunction with dividend payments suggests the company may be financing dividends through debt or asset liquidation, indicating potential liquidity or solvency concerns.
D. The company's equity base of $20 million is sufficient to support the negative free cash flow indefinitely.
questions.tag: Free Cash Flow & Capital Allocation — Sustainability Assessment

questions.text: In examining a wholesale distribution company, a CFE discovers that accounts payable increased by $8.2 million (38% increase) while cost of goods sold decreased by 12% year-over-year. The company's cash position declined by $5.1 million despite this payables increase. Which explanation is most consistent with fraudulent activity?
options: A. The company is negotiating better payment terms with suppliers, legitimately increasing payables while reducing operational costs.
B. The company may be recording fictitious payables or related-party transactions to inflate liabilities and artificially reduce net income for tax purposes.
C. The decline in COGS reflects operational efficiency, while increased payables indicate improved vendor relationships and extended payment terms.
D. The cash reduction is attributable to normal seasonal working capital fluctuations common in the distribution industry.
questions.tag: Accounts Payable Analysis — Liability Manipulation & Related-Party Schemes

questions.text: A CFE analyzing a real estate development company's balance sheet notes that capitalized software development costs increased from $1.2 million to $4.8 million in a single year, while the amortization expense remained minimal. The company's gross margin improved from 28% to 34% during this period. Under the matching principle and asset recognition criteria, what is the most likely conclusion?
options: A. The company has successfully developed proprietary software that enhances operational efficiency, justifying the capitalized investment.

B. The company may be improperly capitalizing ordinary software expenses that should be expensed immediately, thereby inflating assets and overstating net income by deferring legitimate operating expenses.
C. The increase in capitalized costs demonstrates the company's commitment to technology innovation and modernization.
D. The improved gross margin confirms that the capitalized software development has generated the expected operational benefits.
questions.tag: Capitalization vs. Expense Recognition — Improper Deferral of Costs

questions.text: During a fraud examination of a financial services firm, a CFE identifies that the company recognized revenue of $12.5 million from a contract where the customer had explicit rights of return within 24 months and the collectibility of the receivable was uncertain. Under ASC 606 (IFRS 15), which assessment is most appropriate?
options: A. The revenue should be recognized immediately because the contract has been executed and signed by both parties.

B. The revenue recognition should be deferred or adjusted downward because the existence of rights of return and uncertain collectibility violate the criteria for recognizing revenue when control of the good or service has been transferred.
C. The entire amount should be recognized as a contract liability rather than revenue.
D. The transaction is appropriate provided the company establishes a refund liability and asset return obligation.
questions.tag: Revenue Recognition Standards — ASC 606/IFRS 15 Compliance

questions.text: A CFE examining a company's financial records discovers that goodwill on the balance sheet represents 42% of total assets, and the company has not performed an impairment analysis in two years despite a 35% decline in the operating performance of the acquired subsidiary. The company's stock price has also declined 28% over the same period. Which conclusion regarding financial statement integrity is most supported?
options: A. Goodwill impairment testing is required only when explicitly mandated by the SEC, and the company is in compliance.

B. The combination of deteriorated operational performance, declining stock price, and lack of timely impairment testing suggests a significant risk that goodwill is overstated, potentially materially misrepresenting the company's financial position.
C. Goodwill impairment is a discretionary accounting estimate that companies may defer based on management's long-term strategic outlook.
D. The 42% goodwill ratio is reasonable for acquisition-driven growth strategies and does not warrant impairment consideration.
questions.tag: Goodwill & Impairment Testing — Long-Lived Asset Evaluation

questions.text: A CFE is analyzing a company's lease accounting and discovers that a company classified a 10-year lease with a present value of lease payments equal to 89% of the asset's fair value as an operating lease under previous guidance, but has not yet adopted ASC 842 (IFRS 16) requirements. What is the most significant implication for financial statement analysis?
options: A. The company's balance sheet significantly understates assets and liabilities by failing to recognize the right-of-use asset and lease liability, distorting key financial ratios such as debt-to-equity and asset turnover.
B. The treatment is appropriate under historical accounting practices and requires no adjustment for analytical purposes.
C. Operating lease treatment is the preferred method that appropriately matches expenses to revenues.
D. The lease classification decision has no material impact on the company's reported financial position or performance.
questions.tag: Lease Accounting & ASC 842 — Balance Sheet Recognition Impact

questions.text: During a comprehensive fraud examination, a CFE discovers that a company's board of directors is related to a significant vendor that supplies 38% of the company's inventory. The company reports no related-party transaction disclosures in the notes to financial statements, and supplier pricing is 15-22% above market rates. Which combination of factors best suggests potential fraud?
options: A. The pricing premium reflects specialized goods and services that warrant higher costs, and the lack of disclosure is acceptable because the relationship is common knowledge.

B. The absence of related-party disclosures combined with above-market pricing and director involvement indicates potential self-dealing, undisclosed conflicts of interest, and misappropriation of assets.
C. The company's decision to source from a related vendor demonstrates board commitment to relationship building and vendor loyalty.
D. The 15-22% pricing premium is within normal competitive ranges for specialty suppliers and does not require investigation.
questions.tag: Related-Party Transactions & Disclosure — Conflict of Interest Detection

questions.text: A CFE examining a company's statement of changes in stockholders' equity discovers that the company recorded a $6.8 million "adjusting entry" reclassifying amounts from retained earnings to paid-in capital without corresponding changes to net income or other comprehensive income. The company's disclosure notes contain no explanation for this reclassment. Which analytical concern is most appropriate?
options: A. The reclassification is a routine administrative adjustment that reallocates equity components without affecting financial position.

B. The unexplained reclassification from retained earnings to paid-in capital may indicate attempts to manipulate equity ratios, mask losses, or circumvent restrictions on dividend payments tied to retained earnings levels.
C. Reclassifications between equity accounts are standard practice and do not require disclosure or investigation.
D. The adjustment demonstrates proper accounting for stock-based compensation arrangements.
questions.tag: Stockholders' Equity Analysis — Undisclosed Reclassifications & Manipulation

questions.text: A CFE analyzing a company's deferred tax asset (DTA) of $22.4 million discovers that the company has generated cumulative operating losses of $84 million over the past eight years, and market conditions suggest continued losses are likely. Management has recorded a full valuation allowance against 75% of the DTA. Under ASC 740, which assessment most appropriately reflects the quality of this accounting treatment?
options: A. The partial valuation allowance is appropriate and conservative, balancing the uncertainty of future profitability with management's assessment of realization prospects.

B. Management may have inadequately assessed the probability of realizing the remaining 25% of the DTA in light of negative historical performance and uncertain future prospects, potentially overstating the net DTA asset.
C. The full amount of the DTA should be recorded as an asset because tax loss carryforwards provide permanent future benefits.
D. The valuation allowance determination is irrelevant to financial statement analysis because deferred taxes do not affect operating performance.
questions.tag: Deferred Tax Assets & Valuation Allowances — Realizability Assessment
